summary:
This joint resolution directs the President to remove United States Armed Forces from hostilities within or against the Islamic Republic of Iran that have not been authorized by Congress.

The resolution begins with findings asserting that Congress holds the sole constitutional power to declare war, while acknowledging the President's constitutional responsibility to defend the United States, its territories, citizens, servicemembers, and diplomats from attack. It states that Congress has not declared war on Iran or any person or organization within Iran, nor enacted a specific statutory authorization for military force there. It characterizes the use of military force within or against Iran—including enforcing a naval blockade—as the introduction of U.S. Armed Forces into hostilities under the War Powers Resolution.

The operative provision directs the President to remove U.S. Armed Forces from hostilities within or against Iran unless those hostilities are explicitly authorized by a declaration of war or a specific authorization for use of military force. The resolution invokes expedited congressional procedures under section 1013 of the Department of State Authorization Act, Fiscal Years 1984 and 1985, and section 601(b) of the International Security Assistance and Arms Export Control Act of 1976.

The resolution includes a rule of construction stating that nothing in it prevents the United States from: (1) defending against an attack on the United States or its personnel or facilities in other nations; (2) collecting, analyzing, or sharing intelligence about threats from Iran or its proxies with partner countries attacked by Iran since February 28, 2026, and other nations and international organizations; and (3) assisting such partner countries and other nations by helping intercept retaliatory attacks by Iran or its proxies or by providing defensive materiel support for those defensive measures.

The resolution was introduced on April 27, 2026, by Senator Kaine and others, referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations, and on May 19, 2026, the committee was discharged and the resolution placed on the calendar. The text specifies no further effective date.
